title: TIE spawn DEPTH is $7C00 (31,744) — matches unscaled
claim: The ROM STARTING-LOCATIONS macro .WB emits $7C00 as the first (depth) word of every TBG entry (WSCPU.MAC:1181). $7C00 = 31,744 raw ROM units. Our TIE_SPAWN_DISTANCE = 0x7c00 = 31,744 (state.ts:201). Because models.ts is authored in raw ROM units, the distance ports unscaled — exact match. (ROM world-X-forward maps to our −Z by convention; magnitude identical.)
reasoning: The .WB name,_,a,b macro body is `.WORD 7C00 / .WORD a*400 / .WORD b*400`; the depth word is a literal $7C00 shared by all 12 entries. The clone restored this in sw4-1 (it previously carried a compressed 8000). Values are 16-bit, $4000=1.0, so 31,744 ≈ 1.937 in fixed point — the far edge of the ±$7CFF play cube.

ours.verbatim: export const TIE_SPAWN_DISTANCE = 0x7c00 // 31744 — WSCPU STARTING LOCATIONS depth word

title: TIE lateral spawn step is ×$400 (1024 units)
claim: The .WB macro multiplies each lateral operand by $400 (WSCPU.MAC:1182): `.WORD '.3'*400`. $400 = 1024. Our ROM_LATERAL_UNIT = 0x400 = 1024 (sim.ts:1162), and SPAWN_LATERALS multiplies the {0,±1,±2} pattern by it, so laterals are exactly {0, ±1024, ±2048} — the ROM's discrete offsets, unscaled.
reasoning: The two lateral words of each TBG entry are operand×$400. Decoding the 12 .WB rows (WSCPU.MAC:1186–1200): the A/B/C groups each use (0,+1),(−1,0),(+1,0) → {0,±1024}; the D group uses (−2,0),(+2,0),(0,+2) → the ±2048 corners. Ours reproduces this set exactly.

ours.verbatim: const ROM_LATERAL_UNIT = 0x400 // 1024 — the ×$400 STARTING-LOCATION lateral step

title: D-group corner laterals are ±2 units (±2048); only the D group uses them
claim: WSCPU.MAC:1198 `.WB 1D1,0,-2,0` decodes to (depth $7C00, −2×$400 = −$800 = −2048, 0). Our SPAWN_LATERALS 1D1 entry is [-2 * ROM_LATERAL_UNIT, 0] = [-2048, 0] (sim.ts:1173). The ±2048 offset appears ONLY in the D group (1D1/1D2/1D3), matching the ROM — the A/B/C groups never exceed ±1024.
reasoning: Confirms both the value and the placement: our comment and the ROM agree that ±2048 lives only in the D-group. sim.ts:1173–1175 carry 1D1=[-2048,0], 1D2=[2048,0], 1D3=[0,2048], mirroring WSCPU.MAC:1198–1200 (−2,0)/(2,0)/(0,2).

title: Space wave holds a maximum of 3 live TIEs, refilling on death
claim: The space-wave handler PHESP1 keeps up to 3 aliens alive: `CMPA #03 ;3 ALIENS ALIVE?` and, if fewer, `JSR ADASHP` throws another (WSMAIN.MAC:1451–1453). Our WAVE_SIZE = 3 (state.ts:169) and the spawn loop only spawns while `movedEnemies.length < WAVE_SIZE` (sim.ts:210) — the same 3-slot ceiling with refill.
reasoning: ADASHP (WSCPU.MAC:1058) is the mid-wave top-up: it fills the first empty ALIEN slot from the wave list. The clone spawns into a free slot on a timer once below 3. The CEILING (3) is an exact match; the refill trigger differs in mechanism (ROM: immediate whenever <3; ours: on a spawnInterval timer) but the slot count is identical.

title: A regular TIE dies on one hit
claim: The shape table gives the TIE 1 life: `.WS TIE,1` (WSCPU.MAC:1165); the .WS macro stores that byte as # OF LIVES (WSCPU.MAC:1162), loaded into A$HTA at spawn (NWASHP `LDA 1(U) ;DRONES DIE ON N HITS`, WSCPU.MAC:1038). On a hit CPHTSA does `DEC A$HTA / LBLE XPSA` (WSCPU.MAC:362–363), so HTA 1→0 explodes immediately. Our combat loop kills a TIE on the FIRST bolt contact — `killedTie.add(ei)` fires on the first `collides(... TIE_HIT_RADIUS)` (sim.ts:265–266). Match: one hit, one kill.
reasoning: Darth (RTH) has 4 lives (WSCPU.MAC:1166) and never dies in space (see A-016), but the ordinary TIE is a one-hit kill in both. Our sim has no hits-to-kill counter at all — a single collide destroys the TIE — which is behaviourally identical for the 1-life TIE.

title: TIE fire floor 'too close' is $800 (2048 units)
claim: A TIE will not fire when the player's forward range drops to $800: CPUAL does `LDD M.XP / SUBD #800 / BLE 40$ ;B TOO CLOSE` (WSCPU.MAC:629–631). $800 = 2048. Our TIE_NEAR_BOUND = 0x800 = 2048 (state.ts:308) is used as the strafe-fire floor (sim.ts:235 `inPassWindow = ... length(e.pos) > TIE_NEAR_BOUND`). Exact match on the fire floor.
reasoning: This is the ROM's literal 'not too close' gate on TIE gunfire (also cited in the flight-AI model §6 as range MReg00 > $800). The clone recovered the exact $800 in sw4-1. NOTE: ours ALSO reuses this same bound as a peel trigger, which the ROM does not do (see A-011) — but as a FIRE floor the value and role are confirmed identical.

ours.verbatim: export const TIE_NEAR_BOUND = 0x800 // 2048 — WSCPU "not too close" fire/peel floor

title: Per-wave simultaneous-fireball cap column matches TGPROB exactly
claim: TGPROB's third column (guns-allowed) reads 1,1,2,3,4,5,6,6,6,6,6 for wave-hardness indices 0–10 (WSCPU.MAC:738–748; e.g. index 6 is `.PROB 07,020,6 ;6`, WSCPU.MAC:744). Our FIRE_CONCURRENCY = [1,1,2,3,4,5,6,6,6,6,6,6,6,6,6,6] (gameRules.ts:188) reproduces indices 0–10 verbatim, then saturates at 6. Exact match on the ported column.
reasoning: The .PROB macro's third arg (`...C`) is the count of guns allowed to fire that wave (macro WSCPU.MAC:731–734). The table has 11 entries (index 0–10). Our array runs to 16 and holds 6 past index 6; since the ROM clamps its index to the 11-entry table (CPUAL `CMPB #TGPROZ-TGPROB/4`, WSCPU.MAC:636) and the value saturates at 6 from index 6 on, the extra tail is harmless. The clone did NOT port the mask/threshold columns (see A-012) — only this slot-cap column, which it took 1:1.

title: A TIE spawns facing the player
claim: NWASHP orients a fresh alien to face the player: `LDA #0C0 ;FACE ALIEN TOWARDS PLAYER` written into M$AX and M$BY ($C0 → −1.0 on the depth/lateral diagonal) (WSCPU.MAC:1028–1030). Our spawnTie sets `orient: lookRotation(dir)` where dir = toCockpit(pos) (sim.ts:1187,1189) — the model's forward axis is turned onto the cockpit. Same result: the TIE looks at the player at spawn.
reasoning: $C0 is the high byte of $C000 = −1.0 in $4000=1.0 fixed point, flipping the identity orientation's Ax/By to point the model back down-range at the ship (flight-AI model §4 'turned to face the player'). lookRotation(toCockpit) is the float equivalent. Difference: the ROM starts at ZERO velocity and accelerates along that facing; ours starts at full |vel| along it (see A-014) — the FACING at spawn, however, matches.

ours.verbatim: return { pos, vel: scale(dir, speed), kind: 'tie', orient: lookRotation(dir), bank }

title: TIE choreography is a per-fighter bytecode VM; ours is a 2-state hand machine
claim: Each ROM TIE runs a bytecode 'choreography' interpreter (WSCPU.MAC:822–927): a program counter (A$CHPC) over opcodes IF/GOTO/GOSUB/RETURN/TWIRL (TCHOP jump table WSCPU.MAC:862–868), driving scripted maneuver instructions `.CT time,twist,move` (e.g. WSCPU.MAC:1331 `.CT 40,0,C$MF ;TAKES ME HALFWAY TO PLAYER`) gated by event masks `.CUNTIL`/`.CIF` (waits on C$AS 'player in sights', C$PN 'player near', etc.). There are ~13 distinct scripts (TCH1A1…TCH1DZ, WSCPU.MAC:1328–1656) plus a random-split subroutine SPLIT (WSCPU.MAC:1605). Our moveEnemy (sim.ts:1109) is a fixed 2-state machine — approach (constant homing+swoop) then peel — with a single seeded bank constant and NO script, no events, no per-maneuver timing, no subroutines.
reasoning: This is the same shape Tempest's audit found: a real per-entity bytecode VM the clone reduced to a hand-written state machine. Ours is a genuine SIMPLIFICATION, not a structural equal — it cannot express the ROM's event-gated maneuver sequences (turnover, wall-to-wall dashes, partner passes, the C$R1/C$R2 random split) at any constant setting. Faithfully porting it is a VM + script-table rewrite, not a tuning change. The secondary flight-AI model §5.1/§10 flags this as recovered-but-unported.

ours.verbatim: function moveEnemy(e: Enemy, dt: number): Enemy {

refutation_corrections: Script-count precision: the finding says '~13 distinct scripts (TCH1A1…TCH1DZ)'. The TCH1 family actually holds 16 labels — 4 groups (A/B/C/D) × 4 scripts each (x1, x2, x3, and the xZ 'BE MEAN TO PLAYER' loiter loop): TCH1A1/A2/A3/AZ, 1B*, 1C*, 1D*. On top of those sit 12 TCH2* entry points (TCH2A1…TCH2D3) that each `.CGOSUB SPLIT` then fall through into the matching TCH1 script, plus the SPLIT subroutine — so the true authored surface is ~28 entry points over 16+ script bodies, not ~13. This makes the VM strictly LARGER than the finding states; it does not weaken the finding or change the size-l rating.

title: We damage the player on TIE-body cockpit contact; the ROM has no TIE↔player body collision
claim: Our sim costs the player a shield when any TIE reaches the cockpit sphere: `standingEnemies.filter((e) => { if (collides(e.pos, COCKPIT, COCKPIT_HIT_RADIUS)) { damage++; player-death cause 'enemy' ...` (sim.ts:308–315). The ROM has no such collision: TIEs run choreography that loops forever in a 'BE MEAN TO PLAYER' loiter (`.CGOTO 10$`, WSCPU.MAC:1387) and are position-clamped to the play cube (CPCHKL, WSCPU.MAC:799–811); only fireballs damage the player. No alien-body-vs-ship test exists anywhere in the alien code.
reasoning: WSCPU's COLLISION section (WSCPU.MAC:341–446) handles only the PLAYER's laser hitting the alien (CPHTSA→XPSA); there is no reverse test. The choreography scripts all terminate in an infinite loiter loop (TCH1AZ/1BZ/1CZ/1DZ, each ending `.CGOTO 10$`), so a TIE never bee-lines into the cockpit — it maneuvers and shoots, then peels at wave end (A-011). The flight-AI model §7 corroborates: 'only fireballs damage the player.' Our body-collision is a deliberate product deviation (state.ts:288–299 notes the story kept it), but it is a fidelity divergence: faithfully removing it requires the loiter + fireball threat to carry lethality.
refutation_corrections: Recommendation must reconcile with G-010, not stand as an independent 'fix'. A-010 and pair-guns G-010 describe the IDENTICAL behaviour and the identical code (sim.ts:308-315) with opposite dispositions (A-010: fix/remove; G-010: accept). Per coverage-review.md §2b and the code's own record, the correct disposition is ACCEPT: it is a documented, deliberate product deviation (story 9-3 AC#3, state.ts:288-299 'the story deliberately keeps it'), and A-011's per-fighter peel means only a near-dead-center TIE — one whose lateralOffset is inside COCKPIT_HIT_RADIUS=80 (sim.ts:1120) — ever reaches the cockpit sphere at all, so it rarely triggers. The divergence is genuine and worth RECORDING, but A-010 should be demoted to a cross-reference of G-010's accept rather than shipping a conflicting size-m removal task. The ROM fact is CONFIRMED; the 'fix' recommendation is the part that is wrong.

title: TIEs peel away per-fighter at the near-bound; the ROM loiters and retreats only at wave end
claim: Our moveEnemy latches a per-TIE peel the moment a fighter reaches TIE_NEAR_BOUND off-centre: `const peeling = e.peeling ?? (dist <= TIE_NEAR_BOUND && lateralOffset >= COCKPIT_HIT_RADIUS)` (sim.ts:1120), after which it thrusts outward and is culled. In the ROM a TIE never peels during combat — it loiters and keeps firing. Retreat is a WAVE-END event: CPURET recentres each survivor and drives its depth `ADDD #400 ;RACE BACK TO DETH STAR` (WSCPU.MAC:500) per frame until it overruns and its slot frees. CPURET runs only in the PH$SP2 exit phase (WSMAIN.MAC:1473), not per-combat-frame.
reasoning: Two different lifecycles. ROM: all survivors peel together at the descent transition, racing back +$400/frame (= $400×20.508 ≈ 21,000 units/s toward the Death Star). Ours: each fighter independently peels when it individually reaches 2048 units, so the screen empties continuously rather than en masse at wave end. Our peel is a reasonable stand-in for the missing wave-end mechanic (and prevents the full-frame-wall defect), but it is behaviourally distinct. Recommend accept: a faithful version is coupled to the space-phase timer (pair-timing's PH.TIM) — a larger structural change tracked under A-009/A-019.

ours.verbatim: const peeling = e.peeling ?? (dist <= TIE_NEAR_BOUND && lateralOffset >= COCKPIT_HIT_RADIUS)

title: Fire decision is a per-TIE seconds timer; the ROM uses a frame-mask + PRNG + in-view gate
claim: The ROM fires a TIE gun only when a bundle of gates pass: player is in the alien's view (`BITA #C$PV/100 / BEQ`), range > $800, not glowing, a cadence window `LDA FRAME+1 / ANDA 0(U) ;SPACE THEM OUT` (WSCPU.MAC:646–647, mask = $0F/$07/$03 → 1-in-16/8/4 frames), and a PRNG threshold `CMPA 1(U)` (WSCPU.MAC:650). Our fire gate is a per-TIE seconds cooldown plus in-pass-window and the slot cap: `if (inPassWindow && cooldown <= 0 && enemyShots.length < params.maxConcurrentShots)` (sim.ts:236), with cooldown reset to a scalar enemyFireInterval — no view/arc check, no frame-mask, no PRNG roll.
reasoning: UNIT NOTE: the ROM cadence is a free-running FRAME-counter mask, not a reload timer (flight-AI model §6: 'no per-TIE reload timer'). Mask $0F fires at most 1/16 game-frames = 16/20.508 ≈ 0.78 s windows, each gated ~50% by the $80 PRNG threshold → an effective wave-0/1 cadence near ~1.5 s. Our fixed 1.0 s interval (state.ts:232) lands in the right ballpark but the MECHANISM (deterministic cooldown vs stochastic frame-mask, and the missing in-view/arc requirement) diverges. The slot-cap half of the ROM gate IS ported faithfully (A-007). Accept: the simplification plays close; the missing in-arc gate is the notable behavioural gap (our TIEs can fire without the cockpit being in their view frustum).

title: Difficulty is a scalar 1+0.15·(wave−1) ramp; the ROM uses a data-driven hardness index
claim: Our waveParams scales spawnInterval, enemySpeed and enemyFireInterval by `ramp = 1 + (wave−1)·RAMP_PER_WAVE` with RAMP_PER_WAVE = 0.15 (gameRules.ts:165,191). The ROM has no scalar ramp: hardness is an index WV.HRD = min(SP.WAV + GM.DIF, 15) (WSMAIN.MAC:1359–1364 `STA WV.HRD ; CURRENT WAVES DIFFICULTY`) that selects a row of the TGPROB fire table (A-007); enemy SPEED and maneuvers come from the per-wave choreography scripts + composition tables (A-009/A-017), not a multiplier.
reasoning: The clone's speed/spawn/fire-rate ramp is invented (single-sourced 'authentic-FEEL' per gameRules.ts:139–147); the ROM ramps difficulty by (a) the fire-table index and (b) which composition/scripts a wave draws (harder scripts, Darth ordering, more aggressive fire rows). Only the fire-concurrency column is a faithful port (A-007); the scalar enemySpeed/spawnInterval ramp has no ROM counterpart. Accept as a pragmatic stand-in, but note it is not the ROM's difficulty model.

title: Constant approach speed vs the ROM's choreographed per-axis thrust (÷32 / ÷64)
claim: The ROM builds a TIE's per-frame motion by adding orientation basis vectors scaled by ÷64 (SNMVF `JSR ASRD6`, WSCPU.MAC:226) or ÷32 (SNMVF2 `JSR ASRD5`, WSCPU.MAC:211), selected by the choreography move flags (C$MF, C$MF2, C$MU, …). With $4000 = 1.0, forward-once adds $4000÷64 = $100 = 256 units/frame and forward-twice adds $4000÷32 = $200 = 512 units/frame. At the 20.508 Hz game frame: 256×20.508 ≈ 5,250 u/s (C$MF) and 512×20.508 ≈ 10,500 u/s (C$MF2). Our ENEMY_SPEED = 10,000 u/s (state.ts:210) is a single CONSTANT magnitude, ≈5% under the C$MF2 rate and applied as |vel| along a homing heading rather than summed per-axis basis thrusts.
reasoning: UNIT TRAP handled: converting the ROM's per-frame integer thrust at the real 20.508 Hz frame (NOT 60) gives 5,250 / 10,500 u/s for the two forward speeds; our 10,000 sits just below C$MF2, so the MAGNITUDE is roughly faithful. The DIVERGENCE is structural: the ROM speed is quantised and VARIES step-to-step with the active move-flag combination (e.g. C$MF+C$MU, C$MF2, C$MF3=768/frame≈15,750 u/s), rebuilt each frame from the rotating orientation; ours is one fixed scalar on a smooth heading. Had the clone assumed 60 fps for the $200/frame delta it would read 512×60 = 30,720 u/s (≈2.93× too fast) — it did not; it derived 10,000 from a transit-time design target, which happens to land near the true C$MF2 rate. Accept.

title: Spawn walks all 12 TBG slots monotonically; the ROM pairs 3-of-a-group with fixed choreography
claim: In the ROM each wave entry pairs a specific shape, choreography script and begin-location as a triple — `.WV TIE,1A1,1A1` (WSCPU.MAC:1253) — and a wave draws only ONE group's 3 begin-locations (TWV1A uses 1A1/1A2/1A3; TWV1D/TRTH1D use the D-group corners). Our spawnTie instead indexes a flat 12-entry table monotonically: `SPAWN_LATERALS[spawnIndex % SPAWN_LATERALS.length]` (sim.ts:1185), cycling 1A1→1A2→…→1D3 regardless of wave, with NO tie between a lateral and a choreography.
reasoning: The clone recovered the SET of laterals (A-002/A-003) but not their grouping or their pairing with scripts. Consequence: our walk visits the ±2048 D-corners on 3 of every 12 spawns in every wave, whereas the ROM only presents ±2048 when a D-group wave/level is active — so our lateral DISTRIBUTION per wave differs, and the lateral no longer implies a maneuver. This is a facet of the missing composition/choreography system (A-009/A-017); accept until those are ported.
refutation_corrections: FALSE SUB-CLAIM: the reasoning states 'our walk visits the ±2048 D-corners on 3 of every 12 spawns in every wave, whereas the ROM only presents ±2048 when a D-group wave/level is active.' The ROM DOES present the ±2048 D-corners in every wave. TWV2Z (WSCPU.MAC:1299-1320) uses begin-locations 1D1/1D2/1D3 nine times, and TWV2Z is the sustaining group in EVERY space-wave set (SETA1..SETA6, WSCPU.MAC:1230-1235 — SET A1 = TWV1A,TWV2B,TWV2C,TWV2Z is the first wave). So both ours and the ROM show ±2048 corners in the first wave; the asserted per-wave lateral-DISTRIBUTION gap ('ROM shows no D-corners early') is illusory. The genuine divergence is the missing begin-loc<->choreography pairing and the TSPWAV SET structure/order, NOT a ±2048-only-in-D-waves distribution difference.

IMPRECISE: 'a wave draws only ONE group's 3 begin-locations' — a space wave is a TSPWAV SET that CHAINS several TWV groups (SET A1 chains TWV1A,TWV2B,TWV2C,TWV2Z), so a wave draws several groups' begin-locations, and the per-wave lateral set is the union of those groups (which, via TWV2Z, always includes the D-corners).

title: Darth Vader's TIE (RTH): 4 lives, immortal in space, retreats — absent from our sim
claim: The ROM has a distinct Darth ship (RTH shape, `.WS RTH,4` = 4 lives, WSCPU.MAC:1166) that CANNOT be killed in the space wave: on a non-fatal hit CPHTSA resets its hit counter — `LDA #05 / STA A$HTA(X) ;KEEP DARTH ALIVE` (WSCPU.MAC:367–368) — scores it via SCRDARTH, forces a glow/roll, and throws the ship for a loop; it then flees at wave end. Our sim has no Darth enemy at all: Enemy.kind is 'tie' only (state.ts:45), VADER_SCORE (2000) is defined but nothing awards it (state.ts:148–153), and there is no survive/retreat rule.
reasoning: Regular TIEs (HTA=1) explode on the first hit; only Darth (HTA≥2) reaches the KEEP-DARTH-ALIVE fall-through, which pins HTA to 5 after every hit — so Darth is effectively immortal in space, scoring on each hit and retreating rather than dying. This is a whole enemy + survival rule with no counterpart. A future Vader-enemy story would wire VADER_SCORE, the invulnerable-glow, and the retreat; it is a large addition (new enemy type + wave-composition slot, see A-017).

title: TSPWAV per-wave composition table (6 sets, Darth ordering) — absent from our sim
claim: The ROM selects each space wave's contents from a data table TSPWAV of 6 sets (WSCPU.MAC:1218–1235), e.g. `SET A1 TWV1A,TWV2B,TWV2C,TWV2Z` (WSCPU.MAC:1230). Each set lists which TWV wave-groups (and thus which TIE/Darth mix and choreography) appear, in order; past the 6 authored sets the selector recycles the last two with 'DARTH FIRST' on odd waves / 'DARTH SECOND' on even (WSCPU.MAC:980–982,1225–1226). Our sim has no composition data: every space wave is an identical flat spawn of TIEs, and Darth never appears.
reasoning: Wave identity in the ROM is DATA (which groups, in which order, with Darth first/second), not a formula. Our clone reduces every wave to 'spawn TIEs until 6 are killed' (A-019) with a wave-independent lateral walk (A-015). Porting this is a data-table + selector addition sitting on top of the choreography VM (A-009) and the Darth enemy (A-016) — hence large. Recorded so the composition gap is explicit, not silently folded into the difficulty ramp (A-013).

title: Damage roll-burst (~20.34°/frame) when a TIE is hit — absent from our sim
claim: When a TIE is hit and glowing, CPUAL rolls it by a fixed large angle each frame: `LDD #5696. ;20.34 DEGREES` into M.SIN with cos 15362 (WSCPU.MAC:541–544), decrementing the A$ROL counter (set to $1F ≈ 31 frames on hit, WSCPU.MAC:371–372). sin(20.34°)×16384 ≈ 5693 ≈ 5696 (the trailing period makes 5696 DECIMAL). At 20.508 Hz that is 20.34×20.508 ≈ 417°/s for ~1.5 s. Our sim has no per-frame damage roll — a hit TIE is either destroyed (fragment cue) or unaffected; TIE_BANK_ANGLE (0.6 rad ≈ 34°, state.ts:286) is a STATIC lean, not a rate.
reasoning: The 20.34°/frame roll is the visible 'spinning when shielded/hit' tell, tied to Darth's survive-and-glow (A-016) and to any glow window. Ours has no equivalent because our TIEs die on one hit and carry no glow/roll state. Cosmetic-adjacent; accept unless a Darth/shield model is added, at which point this roll rate is the recovered constant to use.

title: Space phase clears on a 6-kill quota; the ROM descends on a music-timed schedule
claim: Our space phase ends when phaseKills reaches SPACE_WAVE_QUOTA = 6 (state.ts:501; sim.ts:843 `s.phaseKills >= SPACE_WAVE_QUOTA`). The ROM does NOT gate the space phase on kills: PHESP1 advances a phase timer PH.TIM each frame and, at a music-sequenced threshold, sets `LDA #PH$SP2 ;GO AHEAD AND DESCEND` (WSMAIN.MAC:1443), keeping ≤3 TIEs alive and refilling on death throughout (A-004). You cannot 'clear' the space wave by shooting N fighters.
reasoning: The ROM's descent is driven by the theme-music timeline (PMTH5/PMTHB/PMDES beats measured in PH.TIM ticks, WSMAIN.MAC:1415–1448), not a body count — killing more TIEs never ends the wave sooner, and killing none still descends on schedule. Ours makes the kill count the sole gate. The exact PH.TIM schedule is pair-timing's to own; recorded here as the wave-PROGRESSION divergence (my scope). Accept: a faithful port re-times the phase and couples to the timer, a change tracked with A-011.
